for removing or uh adding and removing the comps.

You built total number of units, we show you advertised occupancy.

How many are vacant down below here?
You'll see that we let you know if there is a rent special or concession tied to the property or, or, or uh associated to a unit in the property.

Um We also take this step, this uh this is a step further and we do calculate net effective rent and net effective price per foot for you at the property level at the floor plan level and all the way down to the unit level.
And lastly, we give you some transactional data as well.
So the number of uh leases for each property over the last 30 days over the last seven days and the number of leases that each property averages per week.

No.

So the reason we call it this is because we base this percentage off of the known number of vacant units in the property.

So again, this is all publicly available information.
We're taking it directly from the property website.
So we can only account for whatever is made available on that property website.
So essentially this is called advertise in case you know, a property is maybe hiding units, you know, that's more most common in the lease up you don't necessarily show all your availability.
But if they don't, we cannot account for them again, we can only make known where we can, we can only report on what is made known on the property website.

So we're, we're taking the information that they're analyzing and when we're reflecting it back to you,
any questions there, does that make sense?

Are, are, are you worried about occupancy right now?
Are you looking to push rent rates.

I think in terms of internal communication too, whether it be, you know, taking you, you know, some of these uh these pricing strategies and, and, and you know, passing that information down to regionals or down to on site teams.
I think visually, this is a really easy way to explain what's going on, you know, perhaps an on site team, they say, hey, you know, we want to get to 100% occupancy.
Uh We have, you know, two or three units left.
Let's let's lower rates to get us there while you look out at the market.
And it's like, hey, everybody else has 21 bedrooms available or whatever it might be.
We're confident, you know, standing pat and holding our, our rate because we think we can achieve that higher rate.
So just an easy way to kind of communicate as well as understanding what is going on in the market.
Any questions here?
Yeah.

And then ultimately, you know, where are we position compared to everybody else?

Um Plus, you know, having the ability to make changes such as adding our comps, removing them, et cetera that is seen as a definite advantage and, and from, from my perspective, um so just overall it seems like a, a more streamlined uh product platform.

Yeah, the is great for sure, pretty much when we, we include radics in like our monthly financial ownership packets which provide a summary and then we use them to put together reports for development and look at existing properties in a concept where they're looking to develop something.

But what has been a challenge to me is that a lot of time we have a lot of affordable properties in our markets and the affordable homes are in the mix and it really skews the data because it it's hard to distinguish that.
So you have to create like a separate report to exclude those and most affordable are not on the website.
So you don't have to worry about that problem.

Um I think we also, you know, there's a lot of information, information problems with radix where if you have 51 bedrooms available, the same floor plan, are they entering the lowest, the highest the average, you know, it's, you could enter anything.
So you really don't know what, either the leasing team is entering or the other property is entering.
So there's too many opportunities for error.
Yeah, I um II I think, and obviously we're biased.
But what's great about this tool is if you ever want to know like, hey, where's this metric coming from or how are you getting this calculation?
I can trace it back, direct directly to the source.
I can say this is exactly where, where it's coming from.
I pull the website there it is as opposed to your point.
I mean, are they giving you the lowest number?
Are they giving you a fake number?
I mean, it's almost like an arbitrary number at that point.
So, so that's what's nice.
I mean, the consistency across what we, what we're able to show you is is there.
Um So Jennifer's
point about us using this for development, is there a way for us to make a proforma market survey?
So if we have an address of where our new community is going to be built and then we can pull comps to make a report.
Oh yeah.

So that's actually releasing at the very beginning of Q two.

It takes 24 hours.
So not, not a big hang up.
But yeah, it's that, that's for sure what we can do.

So what it is is every single unit or I'm sorry.
Yeah, every single unit that's currently available in your comp set.
So I mentioned earlier, but we do calculate, you know, we do show net effective rent and net effective price per foot at this unit level.

So this metric right here, it shows you the number of rent changes for each unit over the last 30 days.

So I'm assuming once those get stabilized that the uh the revenue tools will pick up even more.

I think a really cool one too.

So I know this wouldn't necessarily be re or uh relevant for, for, for the lease up here.
But what a lot of our Arum customers like this, this specific report for is one whenever they have those weekly or biweekly pricing calls with their Arum rep.
Um, they'll actually have this report open just because you can manipulate that on the fly.

So I know with Aum, it's all aggregated information, you don't necessarily know which comps are doing what, but with apartment IQ since we get so granular, you can see where some of those shifts or trends or anomalies are coming from.
And you can kind of you, you can use our data to really complement and make it even better.
But what our customers like as well is uh is we can use this report to help with pricing renewals.

So down the road whenever that comes up, um the idea here is is if we pretend that like let's say that in your property, you have a bunch of two bedrooms that are up for renewal and uh in, let's say in May.

So rather than pricing those off of historical information or even you know, present day March 13th information, you can take a look out and see how the market's pricing their two bedrooms that are available during the same time frame.
So May to May.

So what you would do here is you would toggle on those two bedrooms and then you go out on the date available and you can highlight all of may you click apply and all the data is gonna populate based on two bedrooms that are available in May.
So right now, there's only four of them that are available, but this is the average price per foot.
This is the average rent price and then down below here, these are the 44, the 42 bedrooms that are available in May.
So kind of pricing more apples to apples with with when you where your renters will be looking.
Um Obviously, you don't wanna over price your units, your renewal price and have your renters start looking somebody up somewhere else.
But at the same time, you don't wanna underprice it and leave, you know, some meat on the bone.
So just making sure you're pricing more appropriately.
What is the true comp only portion?
Yeah, great question.
So a true comp this is this is a
an analysis or I guess AAA metric that we created.
Um It's a unit by unit analysis and essentially what we do is we compare your units versus a unit that a renter most likely would as well.
So which units out there are you most likely to lose a render to um in order for a competing unit to be considered a true comp it has to be thresholds based off of location.
